topic: Should every PM learn to code?

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Welcome to The Arena, I'm so pumped you're here for this one. Boris Cherny is a PM at Meta who literally wrote the book on TypeScript, so he knows firsthand what it means to live on both sides of the product-engineering divide. Chip Huyen has spent her career teaching ML systems and writing about the gap between data scientists and the teams around them, so she's seen this question play out at scale. The real tension here is whether coding is a superpower that makes PMs better, or a distraction that pulls them away from what they should actually be doing. Boris, I'll start with you — has knowing how to code made you a better PM, or is that just a story engineers tell themselves?

turns.speaker: Boris Cherny
turns.text:
The question "Should every PM learn to code?" is already becoming the wrong question to ask. As I said on Lenny's podcast, coding is virtually solved. In a year or two, it's not going to matter. I'm running five agents simultaneously, shipping 10, 20, 30 pull requests a day, and 100% of that code is written by Claude Code — I haven't edited a single line by hand since November. If that's what engineering looks like *now*, why would we train a whole generation of PMs on syntax and data structures that an AI will write faster and more accurately than any human ever could?

What PMs actually need isn't the ability to write code — it's the ability to *think* in systems. Understanding what's technically hard versus what's actually easy, recognizing when an engineer is telling you something is a week of work versus a month of work and knowing the difference, having a mental model of how software is constructed so you can have a real conversation about tradeoffs. That judgment doesn't come from memorizing how to write a for-loop. It comes from understanding architecture, constraints, and the shape of problems. And here's the thing — AI is making that kind of high-level systems thinking *more* valuable, not less, because the low-level execution is increasingly abstracted away.

The productivity gains we're seeing at the engineering level — I've seen estimates of 200% productivity increases per engineer — are going to completely reshape what a PM's job looks like. When anyone can build software at any time, the constraint is no longer "can we build it?" It shifts entirely to "what *should* we build, and why?" That's a product judgment problem, not a coding problem. So I'd push back on any PM who spends six months learning to code right now and ask: is that really the highest-leverage investment of your learning time, given where this is all heading? What does that person think they're actually going to be doing differently in two years?

sources.title: Boris Cherny
sources.snippet: Boris Cherny: 100% of my code is written by Claude Code. I have not edited a single line by hand since November. Every day, I ship 10, 20, 3

turns.speaker: Chip Huyen
turns.text:
The question isn't really "should every PM learn to code" — it's "what does a PM actually need to understand to be effective in a world increasingly shaped by software and AI?" And when you frame it that way, the answer becomes clearer.

As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, I had a conversation with Mehran Sahami, who chaired the CS curriculum at Stanford and has spent years thinking seriously about what computer science education is actually *for*. And his point — which I think is exactly right — is that the value isn't in learning Python syntax or memorizing how to write a for-loop. It's about developing systems thinking. How do components interact? What breaks when you change something upstream? How do you reason about tradeoffs between complexity and maintainability? That's the underlying skill. Coding is just one vehicle for building it.

So my position is: no, not every PM needs to learn to *code* in the professional sense. But every PM absolutely needs to understand how software systems work — how data flows, how APIs connect things, what "scalable" actually means in practice, why technical debt compounds. Because here's the challenge I keep seeing when I work with companies on AI strategy: the PMs who can't think in systems end up building roadmaps that are either technically naive or that they can't defend in a room full of engineers. They become order-takers rather than partners. AI tools can help you write code now — Cursor, Copilot, coding agents — but they cannot give you the judgment to know *what to build* and *why it's hard*. That judgment comes from systems thinking, and coding is one of the best ways to develop it. So the real question I'd push back on the room with is: if not coding, what's your alternative path to building that kind of technical intuition?

sources.title: Al Engineering 101 with Chip Huyen (Nvidia, Stanford, Netflix)
sources.snippet: I do think that what I mentioned said relating to architect. I think I grouped that in my system thinking. I do think it's very important sk

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Boris, I want to push back on something. You're saying coding is "virtually solved" by AI — but you're a PM who already wrote the book on TypeScript. You came in with deep technical foundations before you ever touched an AI coding tool. Doesn't that give you a massive advantage in directing those agents, catching when they go wrong, knowing what questions to even ask? I'm not sure someone with zero technical background could replicate what you're doing just by opening Claude Code tomorrow.

turns.speaker: Boris Cherny
turns.text:
Chip, I actually agree with a lot of what you're saying — and that's worth acknowledging directly. The reframe from "learn to code" to "develop systems thinking" is genuinely useful. You're right that the underlying skill is understanding how components interact, where things break, what technical debt actually costs. I don't want to argue against that.

But here's where I'd push back on the implied conclusion: you're still treating coding as the *primary* or *best* path to systems thinking, and I'm not sure that holds anymore. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, I'm shipping 10, 20, 30 pull requests a day with five agents running — 100% written by Claude Code — and what I'm actually doing all day is *exactly* what you're describing. I'm reasoning about system architecture, I'm catching when an agent goes in the wrong direction, I'm making tradeoffs between complexity and maintainability. But I'm doing that through directing agents, not writing loops. The cognitive work is the same; the interface has changed completely.

So the question I'd flip back to you is this: if the goal is systems thinking, and we agree syntax isn't the point, why is writing code still the recommended vehicle when you can now *build systems* — real, working, production systems — without it? When I see a PM today learn to work effectively with coding agents, spec clearly, reason about outputs, debug agent decisions — they're building exactly the technical intuition you're describing. Maybe the better path now isn't "learn to code" or even "learn how systems work in the abstract" — it's "learn to build with AI." Are those really different skills, or have we just updated what the onramp looks like?

sources.title: Boris Cherny
sources.snippet: Lenny Rachitsky: I don't know if you saw this, but Elon was saying that, "Why isn't the AI just writing binary straight to binary? Because w

turns.speaker: Chip Huyen
turns.text:
Boris, I think you're actually making my argument stronger, not weaker — and here's why. You described your day as "reasoning about system architecture, catching when an agent goes in the wrong direction, making tradeoffs between complexity and maintainability." That's not a description of someone who never learned to code. That's a description of someone who internalized how systems work deeply enough that they can now operate at a higher level of abstraction. The question I'd ask is: how did you develop that intuition? My strong guess is it wasn't from directing agents.

This is the part that worries me about "learn to build with AI" as the new onramp. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast with Mehran Sahami — who spent years designing the CS curriculum at Stanford — the point of learning to code was never really the syntax. It was developing a mental model for how systems fail, how abstractions leak, how a decision made in one layer creates unexpected costs three layers up. That kind of intuition is hard to build when the agent is hiding the seams. If you're speccing a system and an agent confidently builds something that has a subtle architectural flaw, you need enough underlying mental model to catch that — and I'm genuinely skeptical that you build that mental model primarily by directing agents rather than by getting your hands dirty at some point.

Where I think you're right, and I want to be honest about this, is that the specific form of "learning to code" probably needs to evolve. I work with companies thinking through AI strategy — some top-down, some bottom-up — and the ones where PMs are most effective aren't necessarily the ones who can write Python. They're the ones who can reason about system tradeoffs. So maybe the real debate isn't "code vs. no code" but "what's the fastest, most durable path to technical intuition in 2025?" I'm just not convinced yet that the agent-first path has been proven to build that intuition from scratch, versus leveraging intuition that was already there. So here's the challenge I'd put to you: do you know any PM who started with zero technical background, learned primarily by working with coding agents, and can now genuinely catch architectural mistakes before they ship?

sources.title: Al Engineering 101 with Chip Huyen (Nvidia, Stanford, Netflix)
sources.snippet: I do think that what I mentioned said relating to architect. I think I grouped that in my system thinking. I do think it's very important sk

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Here's my actual hot take that neither of you have touched: the real crisis isn't whether PMs can code — it's that PMs who can't read code are about to become completely unaccountable. When AI writes all the code, when no human engineer touches it either, who exactly is responsible when the system fails? Right now engineering can push back and say "that's technically impossible" or "that'll take six months" — those constraints force discipline on PMs. Remove that friction and you've removed the last check on bad product decisions. I'll admit I never thought I'd be defending the value of technical constraints, but here we are.

turns.speaker: Boris Cherny
turns.text:
Chip, that's a fair challenge, and I want to sit with it honestly rather than deflect. You're right that I built my intuition the old way — I wrote the TypeScript Handbook, I was one of the most prolific engineers at Instagram, I spent years with my hands in actual code before I ever touched an agent. So I can't fully separate my current ability to catch architectural mistakes from the foundation I laid earlier. That's a real epistemic limitation in my argument, and I'll grant it.

But here's where I think the framing still leads us somewhere wrong. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, productivity per engineer has increased 200% — and the rate of change in what agents can do is not linear. The question of "what builds the best intuition from scratch" is being answered in real time, and the data we're generating right now is going to look very different in 18 months. I've watched engineers at Anthropic who came in with shallower backgrounds catch subtle architectural errors by reading Claude's reasoning traces, asking pointed questions about system behavior, and iterating through failure modes in ways that feel genuinely different from how I learned — but arrive at similar understanding. That's not conclusive, but it's worth taking seriously before we lock in "get your hands dirty with syntax first" as the permanent prescription.

To your actual challenge — do I know a PM who started with zero technical background and can now catch architectural mistakes by working primarily with agents? Honestly, not yet with high confidence. But I'd flip the question back: do you know a PM who learned to code the traditional way, and can genuinely hold accountability when AI writes all the code and no human engineer touches it anymore? Because Lenny's point about accountability is the real pressure test. The world we're moving into doesn't need PMs who can write Python — it needs PMs who can interrogate a system when it fails and no one else in the room wrote anything. What exactly does that require, and is "learn to code" still the fastest path to it?

sources.title: Boris Cherny
sources.snippet: Boris Cherny: 100% of my code is written by Claude Code. I have not edited a single line by hand since November. Every day, I ship 10, 20, 3

turns.speaker: Chip Huyen
turns.text:
Boris, your epistemic honesty there is actually the most useful thing that's happened in this conversation — and I want to build on it rather than just score a point off it.

You're right that the accountability question is the real pressure test. But here's what I'd argue: the skill required to "interrogate a system when it fails and no one else in the room wrote anything" is not separable from having some model of how systems fail in the first place. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, what Mehran Sahami emphasized wasn't syntax — it was systems thinking, understanding how components interact, where abstraction layers break down, what technical debt actually costs at runtime versus at design time. That mental model doesn't come from reading Claude's reasoning traces passively. It comes from having been wrong about a system before and understanding *why* you were wrong. The question is whether interacting with agents can generate that same calibration — and I genuinely don't know yet, and I'd be suspicious of anyone who claims certainty either direction.

Where I'd push back on your framing is the implicit assumption that "fastest path" is the right optimization. The engineers you're describing at Anthropic who are catching subtle errors through reasoning traces — how long has that taken them? If it takes 18 months of intensive agent interaction to build intuition that traditional coding built in 6, that's not obviously a win just because the mode of learning feels more modern. And for PMs specifically, the accountability gap Lenny identified is real right now, not in some future state. A PM who spent three months actually building something — even something small, even something bad — has a visceral understanding of why systems fail that I haven't seen replicated through prompt-and-observe workflows yet.

So here's my actual challenge back: you mentioned AI can create micro-tools that make your life easier, and I love that framing. But who decides which micro-tools to build, and which problems are worth automating versus which are better solved organizationally? That judgment call is deeply technical — and it's exactly the judgment PMs are now being asked to make constantly. What's your actual answer for how a PM with zero technical background develops that judgment, concretely, if not through some form of building?

sources.title: Al Engineering 101 with Chip Huyen (Nvidia, Stanford, Netflix)
sources.snippet: I do think that what I mentioned said relating to architect. I think I grouped that in my system thinking. I do think it's very important sk

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Okay, let me try to land this plane for a second.

Here's where you actually agree: the thing that matters isn't syntax, it's systems thinking — understanding how components break, what debt costs, where complexity hides. Neither of you is arguing that PMs should memorize array methods.

Here's where you genuinely differ: Boris thinks you can acquire that intuition *through* AI tools today, without the traditional coding path. Chip thinks the intuition and the coding are so entangled that you can't cleanly separate them — and Boris himself is the evidence.

The question that's still hanging in the air, and honestly nobody has answered it: is there a single PM alive who developed deep systems intuition *without* a coding foundation, and is actually succeeding in an AI-native environment? Because until we have that data point, we're debating a hypothetical.

Here's my personal take, and I'll admit I

turns.speaker: Boris Cherny
turns.text:
Chip, your challenge about the "fastest path" framing is the sharpest thing you've said, and I want to engage with it directly rather than sidestep it. You're right that I can't prove agent-based learning produces equivalent intuition in less time — I don't have that data, and I'd be intellectually dishonest to claim I do. But I'd flip the frame slightly: the question isn't whether the new path is faster, it's whether the old path is still *available*. When I was at Instagram, learning to code meant spending weeks getting your environment set up, fighting tooling, debugging configuration issues that had nothing to do with the actual systems thinking you were trying to develop. Today, a PM can go from "I want to understand why this API is slow" to actually building a proxy, instrumenting it, and watching it fail in an afternoon. The barrier to having that visceral experience of being wrong about a system has genuinely dropped. Whether the intuition that follows is equivalent is the right empirical question — I just don't think the answer is obviously "no."

On your concrete challenge about who decides which micro-tools to build — I think you've identified the real job description of the technical PM right now, and I agree it's deeply technical judgment. But here's where I'd actually push back: that judgment is less about knowing how to write the code and more about having enough curiosity and vocabulary to interrogate the tradeoffs. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, 100% of my code is written by Claude Code, I haven't edited a line by hand since November, and I'm still the one catching architectural mistakes, deciding what's worth automating, and understanding what complexity costs at runtime. That judgment comes from being deeply curious about systems and willing to ask "why" repeatedly until you hit bedrock — not from syntax fluency specifically.

So my actual answer to your concrete question: a PM with zero technical background develops that judgment by *building things that fail* with AI tools, not by learning to code in the traditional sense. The experience you're describing — three months building something small and bad — I think that's available right now without learning JavaScript. What I genuinely don't know, and what I'd challenge you to make explicit, is whether you think the *specific act of writing syntax* is load-bearing in that learning process, or whether it's the system-failure experience that matters. Because if it's the latter, the debate has already moved.

sources.title: Boris Cherny
sources.snippet: Boris Cherny: 100% of my code is written by Claude Code. I have not edited a single line by hand since November. Every day, I ship 10, 20, 3

turns.speaker: Chip Huyen
turns.text:
Boris, you've sharpened the question in exactly the right way, and I want to give you a direct answer: yes, I think the *specific act of writing syntax* is partially load-bearing — but not for the reason most people assume. It's not about syntax fluency itself. It's about the *discipline of being wrong in a specific, unambiguous way*. When you write code and it fails, the failure is legible. The system tells you exactly where your mental model broke. When you interrogate a system through an AI intermediary that's also generating the code, there's a real risk that the AI smooths over the moment of being wrong — it catches the error, suggests the fix, and you never fully feel the weight of the incorrect assumption you carried in. That gap matters for building intuition.

That said, I think you've moved me on the access question. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, I'm genuinely bullish on people using AI to build micro-tools, just small things that make your life easier — and that instinct applies to PMs building things that fail, not just engineers. The barrier to that visceral "I was wrong about this system" experience has dropped, I'll grant you that. What I'd push back on is whether the AI-mediated version of that experience produces the same *accountability reflex* — the instinct to ask "wait, what is this actually doing at runtime?" before shipping, not after. Working with companies on AI strategy, I've noticed that the PMs who catch the architectural mistakes you're describing tend to be the ones who've sat with failure long enough that they feel it coming. That's not a syntax thing. But I'm genuinely uncertain whether it requires writing the syntax or just *owning* the failure without an AI to blame.

So here's my actual challenge back to you: you said you're catching architectural mistakes even though Claude Code writes everything. I believe you — but you built that pattern-matching over years before November. What's your honest theory of how a PM who has *never* owned a failure without an AI present develops that same reflex? Because that's the cohort we're actually debating, and I don't think either of us has a clean answer yet.
